summary: DHH announces Rails 7's JavaScript strategy, offering three distinct approaches to replace the aging Webpacker/Turbolinks stack. The default path uses Hotwire (Turbo + Stimulus) served via import maps with no bundling required, which he demonstrates works for production apps like HEY. For teams committed to SPAs and transpilation, jsbundling-rails provides a thin bridge to esbuild, rollup, or Webpack without the Webpacker wrapper. The third option remains Rails as a pure API with a separate front-end repo. He argues this tripartite approach honors Rails' omakase doctrine while maintaining the big tent principle, giving developers clear paths rather than muddy compromises.
key_points: Hotwire + import maps replaces Webpacker/Turbolinks/UJS as the default, eliminating the need for Node tooling, bundling, or transpilation entirely
HEY ships ~60KB of JavaScript versus Gmail's ~3MB, proving this lightweight approach can compete with the heaviest client-side SPAs
Webpacker served as a valuable transition but is now stuck in a 'muddy middle' between no-bundler and full-bundler paths
jsbundling-rails offers a deliberately thin integration with JavaScript bundlers, pushing responsibility for managing JS tooling back to developers rather than wrapping it
The three paths (import maps + Hotwire, JS bundler integration, pure API) give teams clear choices instead of one-size-fits-all compromises
money_quotes: Yet Gmail downloads about three megabytes worth of JavaScript to render its inbox. HEY downloads less than sixty kilobytes.
If you can build a Gmail rival with this stack, and have it be met with broad applaud by tens of thousands of paying customers, you can probably build just about anything with it.
No separate watch process needed for builds, no wrestling with configs, instant reloads, none of the node tooling needed at all, with no overall loss of performance or capability.
Hotwire and import maps will emphatically not be The Answer for everyone. It's a great answer. It's the default answer. But it's not the only answer.
It's sorta stuck in the muddy middle between two clearer paths.
The responsibility for managing and updating dependencies flows to the developer.
Rails has been unapologetically full stack since the beginning.
key_insight: Rails 7 resolves its long JavaScript identity crisis by offering three clearly delineated paths — bundler-free Hotwire as the ambitious default, thin bundler integration for SPA teams, and pure API mode — replacing the muddled middle ground of Webpacker.
